title: Randing: the basic willow weave
subtitle: Weaving a single rod over and under alternate stakes
excerpt: Randing is the foundational weave in willow basketry: one rod woven over and under alternate stakes in a single layer. Once the rhythm of randing is established, the rest of willow weaving follows from it. This tutorial covers preparing the willow, setting the stakes, and working a randed row.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for basketry preparation. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. I (1882) for basket-weave techniques including randing and pairing.

glossaryTerms.definition: A willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes in one continuous pass. The simplest and most common willow weave.
A tight basketry weave using three or more rods worked in sequence, each one stepping over the next. Used at the base-to-side transition and at the rim to add strength.
A basketry weave in which two or more rods are worked together as a single weaving element. Faster than randing and produces a denser weave.
A structural upright rod around which the weaving rods pass. Stakes form the frame of the basket; weavers fill the gaps between them.
A pointed steel rod with a wooden handle used to open a path through the weave to insert a new stake or to pack down a row of weaving.

content.text: . One rod, woven over and under alternate

in a single pass around the basket. The weave is straightforward once the rhythm is established, but getting it right at the start matters: randing that is uneven or too loose will produce a basket that distorts under load.
Randing fills the main body of most round and oval baskets. It is used after the base is complete and the initial
that sets the uprights has been worked. It is faster than waling (which uses three or more rods) and produces a lighter, more open weave. For baskets that need to be denser or heavier,
(two or more rods worked together) is used instead.

Willow for basketry must be soaked before use if it has been dried. Buff willow (partially stripped) needs 3 to 5 days submerged in water to become pliable. Brown willow (unstripped, bark on) needs 7 to 10 days. White willow (fully stripped) needs only 30 to 60 minutes. After soaking, roll the rods in a damp cloth and leave for at least an hour, called mellowing, to allow the moisture to distribute evenly through the rod. Willow that has not mellowed will crack on the outside of curves.

Fresh-cut green willow does not need soaking and is immediately workable. The main advantage of working fresh is that the rods are naturally flexible; the main disadvantage is that a basket made from green willow will shrink slightly as it dries, which may loosen some weavers slightly. Pack the randing tightly while the willow is green to compensate.

Randing requires an odd number of stakes. With an even number, the single randing rod would pass over the same stakes on every circuit and under the same stakes on every circuit, producing a striped rather than a woven pattern. An odd number ensures the rod alternates its over-and-under relationship with each stake on each round. If the basket base has an even number of stakes, prick one additional stake into the weave before beginning the randed body.

Select a rod whose butt end (the thick base of the rod) will start the weave. Insert the butt end of the rod behind a stake, pushing it down between that stake and the adjacent stake in the completed base row beneath. The butt should be pushed down to half its length. The working end extends to the right (for a right-handed weaver).

Take the working end of the rod over the next stake to the right, then under the following stake, then over the next, continuing in the over-and-under rhythm. Keep the rod close to the previous row of weaving; press each over-and-under sequence snugly against the row below with your thumb before passing to the next stake. The rod must press against each stake firmly; a loose randing rod produces a slack basket wall.

When the rod runs out, join the new rod butt-to-tip: bring the tip of the old rod behind a stake and lay the butt of the new rod alongside it, both behind the same stake, with the new butt overlapping the old tip by one stake width. Continue weaving with the new rod. The joined section is covered by the next rows of weaving. Avoid making a join at the same stake on consecutive rows, as the joins will produce a visible column; spread joins evenly around the basket.
Each rod lies neatly against the one below it, with no gaps and no looping over the top of the stake. The stakes remain straight and upright. A

can be used to press down any rod that has not seated firmly against the row below. The surface of the basket should be smooth and consistent when you run a hand across it: no rods standing proud of their neighbours.

items.symptom: The randing produces stripes rather than an interlocked weave
items.cause: The basket has an even number of stakes. The same rod passes over the same stakes on every circuit.
items.fix: Prick an additional stake into the base weave to make the count odd. Remove the completed randing, add the stake, and restart.

items.symptom: The rod cracks on the outside of a curve
items.cause: The willow was not soaked or mellowed long enough before use.
items.fix: Soak the remaining rods for a further day and re-mellow for an hour before continuing. Cracked rods should be cut out and replaced.

items.symptom: The basket wall leans outward or inward as randing progresses
items.cause: The stakes are not being held at a consistent angle. Outward lean means the randing is being worked too loose; inward lean means it is too tight.
items.fix: Check the stake angle after each circuit. Use a template ring at the correct diameter to hold the stakes at the right angle while the randing is worked.
